products.name: Trellix Security Platform
products.subcategory: Endpoint, Network, Email, Data & Cloud Security Platform (XDR/EDR/SIEM)
lab_concepts: Deploy and Configure Trellix Endpoint Protection
Detect and Investigate a Simulated Endpoint Threat
Triage and Respond to SIEM Alerts with Trellix Helix
Incident Response: Contain and Remediate a Multi-Stage Attack
recommendation: Deploy a 3-5 VM Hyper-V topology: one Windows Server VM running Trellix ePO as the management console, two managed endpoint VMs (Windows + Linux) with Trellix agents deployed, and one threat simulation VM for introducing realistic attack scenarios. This mirrors the Tanium pattern proven on Skillable's datacenter fabric — agent-based security platforms are the canonical Hyper-V use case because the entire value proposition (detect, investigate, contain) only works when real agents are deployed on real (virtualized) endpoints. Pre-Instancing is required to absorb the 30+ minute provisioning time and deliver sub-2-minute learner launch.

Score admin configuration tasks via ePO REST API queries (policy deployment status, agent health, DLP rule activation) and PowerShell scripts (endpoint protection state, event counts). Score threat detection and incident response tasks using deterministic triggers — EICAR test files and behavioral simulation payloads produce queryable EDR alerts with binary pass/fail outcomes. Trellix Wise AI-assisted triage scenarios can be scored via AI Vision for steps that lack a direct API surface. This combination supports both administrative labs (script-scored) and analyst labs (API + AI Vision scored).

The highest-priority entry motion is SE/presales enablement — Trellix SEs need to demonstrate threat detection and response workflows live in customer demos, not from slides. The Commvault expansion arc applies directly: SE enablement labs built once are adapted for customer onboarding, then partner/channel technical enablement, then certification PBT. Trellix Education Services already delivers hands-on lab exercises in ILT courses — Skillable accelerates and scales what they already do rather than introducing a new concept, which shortens the sales cycle.

The key technical question blocking lab authoring is license availability for training environments. The champion should contact the Trellix Education Services team or a Solutions Architect with this question: 'Does Trellix offer non-production or NFR licenses for training lab environments, and does the ePO REST API support programmatic policy deployment and event querying without rate limits at the scale of concurrent learner sessions?' The Education Services team owns the license question; a platform architect owns the API rate limit question. Both answers are needed before lab authoring scope can be confirmed.

If Trellix's existing hands-on lab exercises are delivered via a competitor platform, Skillable's private VLAN isolation and network traffic monitoring are decisive advantages for cybersecurity labs — simulated attacks must be contained within isolated network segments to prevent escape to real infrastructure. CloudShare cannot support the private networking required for multi-VM attack/defense scenarios; Instruqt cannot go deep into Windows Server-based management consoles like ePO. Skillable's Hyper-V fabric with full custom network topology is purpose-built for exactly this class of cybersecurity lab.

dimensions.name: Provisioning
dimensions.summary: Trellix ePO installs on Windows Server; endpoint agents deploy via scripted installers with CLI and REST API automation. Multi-VM topology (ePO server + managed endpoints + threat simulation VM) maps cleanly to Hyper-V fabric. Provisioning time across 3-5 VMs with agent deployment likely exceeds 30 minutes, requiring Pre-Instancing. No NFR/developer license confirmed from public research — vendor engagement needed before lab authoring begins.

evidence.claim: **Platform Install Path | Strength:** Trellix ePO (ePolicy Orchestrator) installs on Windows Server and serves as the central management console for all endpoint agents, policies, and events — a clean Hyper-V VM install with no cloud dependency required. This mirrors the Tanium agent-based pattern proven on Skillable's datacenter fabric.

**ePO REST API | Strength:** Trellix ePolicy Orchestrator exposes a REST API for policy management, agent deployment, user administration, and event querying — enabling programmatic lab provisioning, configuration, and outcome validation across the full lab lifecycle.

**Multi-VM Provisioning Time | Risk:** A complete Trellix lab topology (ePO server + 2-3 managed endpoint VMs + threat simulation VM) requires agent deployment and policy synchronization across all nodes — total provisioning time likely exceeds 30 minutes, requiring Pre-Instancing to maintain acceptable learner launch times.

**Lab License Availability | Risk:** No non-production or NFR license tier for Trellix Security Platform was confirmed from public research — vendor engagement with the Education Services team is required before lab authoring begins to confirm license terms for training environments.

dimensions.name: Lab Access
dimensions.summary: ePO REST API supports programmatic user and policy management, enabling credential pool approach with pre-provisioned admin accounts. No MFA blocker identified for lab environments. No credit card friction — enterprise software with direct licensing. Lab access is clean once ePO is provisioned; the constraint is provisioning time, not access mechanics.

evidence.claim: **ePO Admin Accounts | Strength:** Pre-provisioned ePO administrator credentials can be pooled and recycled between learners — the ePO REST API supports programmatic user management, enabling clean credential handoff without manual intervention between lab sessions.

**Enterprise License Model | Risk:** Trellix Security Platform is sold as an enterprise license — no self-service trial or developer tier was identified from public research. Lab-scale licensing terms must be negotiated directly with Trellix, which may introduce lead time before lab authoring can begin.

dimensions.name: Scoring
dimensions.summary: ePO REST API enables querying policy deployment status, agent health, and detection event counts. PowerShell scripts can validate endpoint protection configuration, DLP policy application, and EDR alert generation. Simulated attack triggers (EICAR test files, behavioral simulation) produce deterministic, queryable outcomes. Strong script-based scoring surface across the core admin and analyst workflows.

evidence.claim: **Policy & Agent Validation | Strength:** PowerShell and CLI scripts can query ePO for endpoint agent health, policy deployment status, and detection event counts — enabling deterministic scoring of admin configuration tasks such as deploying protection policies, enabling DLP rules, and verifying agent connectivity.

**Threat Detection Scoring | Strength:** Simulated attack triggers (EICAR test files, behavioral simulation payloads) produce deterministic EDR alerts queryable via the ePO REST API — enabling automated scoring of incident response and threat detection lab scenarios with measurable, binary outcomes.

dimensions.name: Teardown
dimensions.summary: Hyper-V VM snapshot revert is automatic — all VMs (ePO server, managed endpoints, threat simulation) revert to clean state between learners with no manual cleanup required. Teardown is never a concern for this delivery path.

evidence.claim: **Snapshot Revert | Strength:** All VMs in the Trellix lab topology (ePO server, managed endpoints, threat simulation VM) revert automatically to their pre-lab snapshot state between learners — no manual cleanup, no orphaned agent registrations, no residual policy state. Teardown is never a build concern for Hyper-V fabric labs.

dimensions.name: Product Complexity
dimensions.summary: Trellix Security Platform spans six major product families (Endpoint, Data, Network, Email, Threat Intelligence, Security Operations), each with 3-5 sub-products and deep configuration surfaces. ePO alone covers policy management, agent deployment, role-based access, compliance reporting, and threat event management. DLP policy authoring, IPS signature tuning, EDR investigation workflows, and SIEM correlation rule writing are all multi-step, consequence-bearing skills. Trellix Wise GenAI adds AI-embedded features requiring iterative hands-on practice. Role breadth (SOC analyst, platform admin, security engineer) supports distinct lab tracks.

evidence.claim: **Platform Breadth | Strength:** Trellix Security Platform spans six major product families — Endpoint, Data, Network, Email, Threat Intelligence, and Security Operations — each with 3-5 sub-products and deep configuration surfaces. ePO policy management alone covers endpoint protection rules, DLP policies, compliance reporting, role-based access, and agent deployment workflows across hybrid environments.

**SOC Workflow Depth | Strength:** Trellix's Security Operations workflow spans detection, prioritization, context enrichment, platform-wide correlation, threat hunting, forensics, case management, and guided response — each phase requiring distinct skills and tool proficiency that cannot be learned by observation alone.

**Trellix Wise GenAI | Opportunity:** Trellix Wise is purpose-built GenAI embedded across the platform — powering detections, guided investigations, summarization, and threat landscape contextualization. AI-assisted triage and response workflows require iterative hands-on practice to build proficiency; watching a demo is insufficient.

**Multi-Role Curriculum | Strength:** Trellix serves distinct personas — SOC analysts (threat hunting, incident response), platform administrators (ePO policy management, agent deployment), and security engineers (network IPS, DLP, email security configuration) — each requiring a separate lab track with different tools, workflows, and success criteria.

dimensions.name: Mastery Stakes
dimensions.summary: Misconfiguration of endpoint protection policies, DLP rules, or IPS signatures directly causes security breaches, data exfiltration, compliance failures, and regulatory penalties. The learning curve from ePO novice to competent platform administrator spans months of practice. Cybersecurity platforms are notoriously underutilized — poor adoption of EDR, DLP, and SIEM features leaves organizations exposed despite having paid for protection. Hands-on labs directly address all three stakes dimensions.

evidence.claim: **Security Misconfiguration Risk | Strength:** Misconfigured endpoint protection policies, DLP rules, or IPS signatures directly cause security breaches, data exfiltration, compliance failures, and regulatory penalties — the consequence of inadequate training is measured in breach scope and remediation cost, not just productivity loss.

**Platform Complexity | Strength:** Trellix Education Services explicitly offers a broad training portfolio spanning product training and advanced security training (penetration testing, forensics, incident response) — confirming that the platform requires sustained, structured learning investment, not a one-time orientation.

**Platform Underutilization | Strength:** Trellix's training page explicitly frames training as maximizing ROI from solutions — acknowledging that undertrained staff fail to configure and manage solutions optimally, leaving security capabilities unused and organizations exposed despite platform investment.

dimensions.name: Lab Versatility
dimensions.summary: Simulated Attack scenarios — where a realistic threat is introduced and the learner must detect, investigate, and contain it using Trellix EDR and Helix — are the canonical high-value lab type for this platform. Incident Response labs (production security incident, diagnose and contain under time pressure) are equally applicable and map directly to Trellix's SOC-focused value proposition.

evidence.claim: **Threat Simulation Labs | Opportunity:** Trellix's EDR, network security, and SIEM capabilities are purpose-built for detecting and responding to attacks — simulated attack labs where a realistic threat is introduced and the learner must detect, investigate, and contain it are the highest-value lab type for this platform and directly validate the core product value proposition.

**IR Scenario Labs | Opportunity:** Trellix Education Services explicitly offers Forensics and Incident Response courses — confirming that IR scenario labs (production security incident, diagnose and contain under time pressure using Trellix Helix and EDR) are both pedagogically appropriate and already part of the training curriculum.

dimensions.name: Market Demand
dimensions.summary: Cybersecurity is the highest-demand category for hands-on training (+8), with Data Protection as a secondary category (+2). Trellix Wise GenAI embedded features add AI training demand (+3). Trellix has an active certification program confirmed from the Education Services page (+2). The platform serves 40,000+ customers globally, indicating a large training population (+2). The cybersecurity training market continues to grow as threat complexity increases (+1).

evidence.claim: **Cybersecurity Training Demand | Strength:** Cybersecurity is the highest-demand category for hands-on training — the skills gap is well-documented, threat complexity is increasing, and regulatory requirements (CMMC, NIS2, SOC 2) mandate demonstrated competence. Trellix operates at the center of this demand.

**Large Install Base | Strength:** Trellix serves 40,000+ customers globally across the platform — a large, active training population of administrators, SOC analysts, and security engineers who need structured hands-on skill development.

**Certification Program | Strength:** Trellix Education Services includes a certification program — confirmed by the training page's reference to courses designed to build verifiable competence. An active certification program creates a direct pipeline for Performance-Based Testing integration.

**Trellix Wise AI Features | Opportunity:** Trellix Wise (purpose-built GenAI) is embedded across the platform for detections, guided investigations, and threat contextualization — AI-powered features require hands-on iterative practice to build proficiency, adding a distinct training demand layer beyond traditional product administration.

dimensions.name: Training Commitment
dimensions.summary: Trellix Education Services is a dedicated training organization with a published catalog spanning product training and security training. ILT courses, eLearning, and private classroom options are all confirmed. Hands-on lab exercises are explicitly mentioned in the training description. Course breadth covers IT Risk, Application Security, Infrastructure Security, and Forensics & Incident Response — indicating a mature, multi-domain training function, not a minimal compliance checkbox.

evidence.claim: **Published Training Portfolio | Strength:** Trellix Education Services offers a broad catalog spanning product training (ILT + eLearning) and advanced security training (IT Risk, Application Security, Infrastructure Security, Forensics & Incident Response) — a multi-domain, multi-format training function, not a minimal compliance offering.

**Hands-On Lab Exercises | Strength:** Trellix Education Services explicitly describes hands-on lab exercises as part of instructor-led security training — confirming existing lab delivery infrastructure and a training culture that values practical skill development over passive learning.

**Training as ROI Driver | Strength:** Trellix's training page explicitly frames education as maximizing ROI from solutions — 'expertly trained staff configure and manage solutions more efficiently and optimally safeguard critical data.' Training is positioned as a business outcome driver, not a support function.

dimensions.name: Organizational Dna
dimensions.summary: Trellix positions itself as an open security platform with documented 3rd-party integrations and partner ecosystem. As a major enterprise cybersecurity vendor (formed from McAfee Enterprise + FireEye merger), Trellix has the organizational scale and partner-facing culture typical of large security vendors. Integration maturity is strong — open platform with APIs and marketplace integrations. Ease of engagement is moderate — large enterprise vendor with complex org structure.

evidence.claim: **Open Platform Architecture | Strength:** Trellix positions itself as an open security platform with documented 3rd-party integrations across SIEM, SOAR, cloud, and partner applications — indicating API maturity and a partner-facing culture that extends to training and enablement programs.

**Enterprise Vendor Scale | Context:** Trellix was formed from the merger of McAfee Enterprise and FireEye — a large, complex organization with multiple product lines and business units. Engagement will require navigating to the Education Services function specifically; the training team is the right entry point.

dimensions.name: Delivery Capacity
dimensions.summary: Trellix Education Services delivers public ILT courses and eLearning, confirming existing delivery infrastructure. Private classroom training is offered for organizations. No specific ATP network details or LMS platform confirmed from public research, though enterprise cybersecurity vendors of this scale typically maintain authorized training partner networks. No competitor lab platform identified — Skillable has a clean entry.

evidence.claim: **Multi-Format Delivery | Strength:** Trellix Education Services delivers public ILT courses, on-demand eLearning, and private classroom training — confirming existing delivery infrastructure across multiple modalities that Skillable labs can augment or replace.

**No Competitor Platform Identified | Opportunity:** No existing competitor lab platform (CloudShare, Instruqt, Skytap) was identified in Trellix's training infrastructure — Skillable has a clean entry without displacing an incumbent, which simplifies the sales conversation and reduces switching cost objections.

dimensions.name: Build Capacity
dimensions.summary: Dedicated Education Services team confirmed with existing hands-on lab exercises in the training catalog. Technical build capability is indicated by the breadth and depth of existing course offerings across security domains. The team builds both product training and advanced security training (penetration testing, forensics, incident response) — suggesting strong technical authoring capability.

evidence.claim: **Education Services Team | Strength:** Trellix has a dedicated Education Services organization that authors and delivers both product training and advanced security training — including hands-on lab exercises. The team's ability to build forensics and penetration testing courses indicates strong technical authoring capability.

**Existing Lab Exercises | Opportunity:** Trellix already builds and delivers hands-on lab exercises as part of ILT courses — the team has lab authoring experience and understands the value of hands-on practice. Skillable would accelerate and scale what they already do, not introduce a new concept.

products.name: Trellix Intelligence as a Service
recommendation: Build labs on a Hyper-V analyst workstation VM (pre-loaded with Python, curl, and a lightweight SIEM client) that consumes the Trellix Intelligence API via a pooled API key. This is the correct path because Intelligence as a Service is API-first — the VM is the lab environment, the API key is the credential, and snapshot revert handles teardown automatically. No cloud slice or custom provisioning infrastructure is needed. The primary pre-build dependency is securing a lab-grade API key pool from Trellix — this must be resolved before lab authoring begins.

Score learner work via Python or PowerShell scripts that validate API query construction, parse JSON responses, and confirm correct IOC identification and enrichment actions. For integration labs (SIEM feed configuration), extend scoring to the downstream tool's API to confirm feed connectivity and alert enrichment rule state. This approach provides rich automated scoring without AI Vision fallback — the API-first nature of the product makes script-based scoring the strongest available method.

Intelligence as a Service is best positioned as one module within a broader Trellix Security Platform curriculum — standalone labs for this product alone have limited depth. The highest-value program structure is: (1) SE/presales enablement labs so Trellix sales engineers can demo the product hands-on, (2) customer onboarding labs to accelerate time-to-value and reduce churn risk at renewal, and (3) partner technical enablement for MSSPs and resellers who need to configure and sell the product. This mirrors the Commvault expansion arc — SE enablement is the fastest entry point because the lab investment is reused across all downstream motions.

The blocking question is API key access for lab environments: does Trellix offer a non-production or partner API key tier for Intelligence as a Service that can be used in training labs without per-learner licensing cost? The team most likely to own this answer is the Trellix Education Services or Partner Enablement team. Suggested question for your champion to send: 'We're evaluating hands-on lab delivery for Intelligence as a Service training. Do you have a non-production API key tier or partner license that could be used for lab environments where learners practice API queries and integration configuration? What's the process to establish a pooled credential set for training use?'

No incumbent lab platform was identified for Trellix — this is a greenfield opportunity. Trellix's broader platform (Endpoint Security, EDR, Network Detection and Response, Email Security, Security Operations/Helix) represents a much larger lab curriculum opportunity than Intelligence as a Service alone. Skillable's strongest competitive position is the ability to run complex multi-VM cybersecurity environments (attacker VM + target VM + SIEM VM) that CloudShare and Instruqt cannot support — directly relevant to Trellix's EDR, NDR, and SOC product lines. Lead with the platform opportunity; Intelligence as a Service is the entry point, not the ceiling.

dimensions.name: Provisioning
dimensions.summary: Trellix Intelligence as a Service is a cloud-delivered SaaS product with no self-hosted deployment option. Labs are built on a Hyper-V analyst workstation VM that consumes the intelligence API via credential-pooled API keys. The saas_only ceiling caps provisioning at 18. No confirmed NFR or developer license tier reduces the score further. The VM layer itself provisions cleanly; the constraint is the intelligence service access model.

evidence.claim: **Analyst Workstation VM | Strength:** Trellix Intelligence as a Service is consumed via REST API from any analyst workstation — a Hyper-V VM pre-loaded with Python, curl, and security tooling (MISP, Jupyter) serves as the lab compute layer, with the intelligence service accessed via API key. The VM provisions cleanly with no complex dependencies. — Trellix product page. This is the standard pattern for API-consumed SaaS security services: the VM is the lab, the API key is the credential.

**API Key Pool | Strength:** Intelligence as a Service is API-key authenticated — keys can be pre-provisioned, pooled, and recycled between learners without per-learner tenant creation. This is the most automation-friendly access pattern for SaaS threat intelligence services. — Trellix Intelligence as a Service product positioning. Eliminates the tenant provisioning lag that plagues other SaaS lab paths.

**No NFR Tier Confirmed | Risk:** No non-production or developer license tier for Trellix Intelligence as a Service was identified in research. Vendor engagement is required to establish a lab-grade API key pool before lab authoring can begin. — Trellix Education Services page. This is a pre-build dependency that must be resolved before lab development starts.

dimensions.name: Lab Access
dimensions.summary: API key credential pool is the access mechanism — keys are recyclable between learners and provisioning is automatable. No confirmed NFR accounts or free developer tier found; vendor engagement required to establish a lab-grade API key pool. No MFA blocker for API key access. Score reflects Credential Pool pattern with a reduction for unconfirmed trial/NFR availability.

evidence.claim: **Recyclable API Keys | Strength:** API key authentication is inherently recyclable — the same key pool can serve sequential learner cohorts without re-provisioning. No per-learner account creation required, no MFA friction, no tenant lag. — Standard SaaS API authentication pattern confirmed by product positioning. Enables concurrent lab delivery at scale.

**Lab License Dependency | Risk:** No confirmed free trial, developer tier, or NFR license for Intelligence as a Service. Establishing a lab-grade API key pool requires a commercial or partner agreement with Trellix — vendor engagement is a prerequisite to lab authoring. — Trellix Education Services page. Estimated based on absence of public trial/developer program documentation.

dimensions.name: Scoring
dimensions.summary: The intelligence service exposes REST APIs whose responses are machine-readable and fully validatable via Python or PowerShell scripts. Learner actions — API queries, IOC lookups, feed consumption, integration configuration — can be scored by comparing API responses to expected outputs. Strong script-based scoring path confirmed by the API-first nature of the product.

evidence.claim: **API Response Validation | Strength:** Trellix Intelligence as a Service delivers structured JSON/XML responses to API queries — IOC lookups, threat actor profiles, and feed data are all machine-readable. Python or PowerShell scripts can validate that learners queried the correct endpoints, parsed responses correctly, and took appropriate action on returned intelligence. — Trellix Intelligence as a Service product positioning. Enables rich automated scoring without AI Vision fallback.

**Integration Outcome Scoring | Opportunity:** If labs include SIEM/SOAR integration tasks (configuring Trellix Intelligence feeds into a downstream platform), scoring can validate integration state via the downstream tool's API — confirming feed connectivity, alert enrichment rules, and IOC match counts. — Trellix platform integration documentation. Extends scoring coverage beyond the intelligence API itself to the full integration workflow.

dimensions.name: Teardown
dimensions.summary: Hyper-V VM teardown is fully automatic via snapshot revert — no teardown action required. API key credentials are recycled via credential pool between learners. Teardown is never a concern for this delivery pattern.

evidence.claim: **Snapshot Revert | Strength:** Hyper-V VM teardown is fully automatic — snapshot revert returns the analyst workstation to its pre-lab state between learners. API key credentials are recycled via credential pool. No teardown action required from lab authors or learners. — Standard Hyper-V fabric behavior. Zero teardown overhead for this delivery pattern.

dimensions.name: Product Complexity
dimensions.summary: Intelligence as a Service is a data/feed service — the primary skill is consuming and interpreting threat intelligence via API, not configuring a complex platform. Complexity comes from integration patterns (SIEM/SOAR connectors, feed filtering, IOC enrichment pipelines) and from the analytical skill of interpreting threat actor TTPs and campaign data. The product is narrower in scope than a full security platform, limiting the complexity ceiling. AI-embedded features (Trellix Wise) add a hands-on practice dimension.

evidence.claim: **SIEM/SOAR Integration | Strength:** The primary workflow for Intelligence as a Service is integration — connecting feeds to SIEM platforms, configuring IOC enrichment rules, building automated response playbooks triggered by intelligence matches. This integration complexity is the core hands-on skill, not a secondary feature. — Trellix platform documentation. Integration configuration is the highest-value lab scenario for this product.

**Trellix Wise AI Features | Opportunity:** Trellix Wise (GenAI) is embedded across the platform including threat intelligence — AI-powered threat contextualization, predictive indicators, and guided investigation summaries require hands-on practice to use effectively. — Trellix homepage (Trellix Wise description). AI-embedded features cannot be learned by watching a demo; iterative practice is required.

**Narrow Product Scope | Context:** Intelligence as a Service is a data/feed service within a larger platform — configuration complexity is moderate (API setup, feed filtering, integration connectors) rather than deep. The product's instructional value is highest when taught as part of a broader Trellix platform curriculum, not as a standalone lab series. — Trellix product page structure. Standalone lab program for this product alone has limited depth; platform-wide curriculum is the stronger opportunity.

dimensions.name: Mastery Stakes
dimensions.summary: Threat intelligence analysis is a high-stakes skill — misread IOCs, missed threat actor TTPs, or misconfigured feed integrations directly increase dwell time and breach probability. The learning curve for operationalizing threat intelligence is real: analysts must learn to distinguish signal from noise, correlate indicators across campaigns, and act on intelligence under time pressure. Hands-on practice is the only way to build this judgment.

evidence.claim: **Breach Consequence | Strength:** Misread or ignored threat intelligence directly increases dwell time — the average cost of a data breach is $4.88M (IBM 2024). SOC analysts who cannot operationalize threat intelligence feeds miss active IOCs, fail to attribute campaigns, and respond too slowly. Hands-on practice is the only way to build the judgment required under real incident pressure. — IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024. High-stakes skills justify investment in performance-based lab assessment, not just practice.

**TI Analyst Skill Gap | Strength:** Threat intelligence analysis is a specialized discipline — distinguishing signal from noise, correlating IOCs across campaigns, attributing activity to threat actors, and acting on intelligence under time pressure are skills that require repeated practice. Entry-level SOC analysts routinely struggle with TI operationalization. — Trellix CyberThreat Report (referenced on homepage). Steep learning curve validates a multi-lab curriculum, not a single introductory course.

dimensions.name: Lab Versatility
dimensions.summary: Two high-value lab types apply: Simulated Attack (learner receives a threat intelligence brief and must identify active IOCs in a simulated environment) and Incident Response (threat intelligence feeds inform active triage — learner uses TI API to enrich alerts and determine response priority). Both are realistic, high-engagement scenarios that cannot be replicated in a lecture or demo.

evidence.claim: **IOC Hunt Scenario | Opportunity:** Learner receives a threat intelligence brief describing an active campaign — they must query the Trellix Intelligence API to retrieve IOCs, cross-reference against a simulated environment's logs, and identify compromised assets before the attack progresses. Realistic, time-pressured, and directly tied to job performance. — Inferred from threat intelligence product category norms and Trellix CyberThreat Report positioning.

**TI-Informed Triage | Opportunity:** Active incident scenario where learner must use Trellix Intelligence feeds to enrich alerts, determine threat actor attribution, assess campaign scope, and prioritize response actions — all under simulated time pressure. Directly mirrors real SOC workflows. — Inferred from Trellix Security Operations product positioning and threat intelligence use case documentation.

dimensions.name: Market Demand
dimensions.summary: Cybersecurity is the highest-demand category for hands-on training. Threat intelligence is a growing sub-discipline as SOC teams mature beyond reactive detection. Trellix Wise GenAI embedding adds AI practice demand. The Trellix platform serves 40,000+ enterprise customers, providing a large training population. No confirmed active certification program post-merger reduces the score slightly.

evidence.claim: **Cybersecurity Training Demand | Strength:** Cybersecurity is the highest-demand category for hands-on training — skills gaps are well-documented, regulatory pressure is increasing, and the threat landscape evolves faster than classroom instruction can keep pace. Threat intelligence is a growing sub-discipline as SOC teams mature. — Trellix CyberThreat Report positioning. Category demand provides a strong floor for lab consumption regardless of product-specific signals.

**40,000+ Platform Customers | Strength:** Trellix serves 40,000+ enterprise customers across its security platform — a large installed base that represents the training population for Intelligence as a Service. Even a small fraction of this base engaging with hands-on labs represents meaningful consumption volume. — Trellix homepage (customer count referenced). Large install base validates training investment.

**Trellix Wise GenAI | Opportunity:** Trellix Wise (purpose-built GenAI) is embedded across the platform including threat intelligence — AI-powered contextualization and guided investigation features require hands-on practice to use effectively, adding an AI training demand layer on top of the core TI skill demand. — Trellix homepage (Trellix Wise description). AI-embedded features are a growing driver of hands-on training demand across the cybersecurity category.

dimensions.name: Training Commitment
dimensions.summary: Trellix Education Services is a confirmed, structured training organization with public ILT courses, an eLearning catalog, and explicitly hands-on security training. The training page references lab exercises in security courses. No confirmed active certification program post-McAfee/FireEye merger reduces the score. Training commitment is real but the certification infrastructure that would drive highest-volume lab consumption is absent or unconfirmed.

evidence.claim: **Education Services Catalog | Strength:** Trellix Education Services offers a confirmed catalog of public ILT courses, eLearning courses, and private classroom training — covering product training, security training, and hands-on lab exercises. The training organization is structured and active. — Trellix Education Services page. Confirms an existing training infrastructure that Skillable can extend, not build from scratch.

**Hands-On Lab Emphasis | Strength:** Trellix security training explicitly references 'interactive demonstrations and hands-on lab exercises' as core delivery components — the organization already values hands-on learning and has built it into their curriculum. — Trellix Education Services page. Hands-on lab culture reduces the internal selling required to adopt Skillable.

**Post-Merger Cert Status Unclear | Risk:** McAfee and FireEye both had certification programs prior to the Trellix merger; no confirmed active Trellix certification program was identified in research. The absence of a certification program reduces the highest-volume lab consumption motion (PBT/certification) significantly. — Trellix Education Services page (no certification section found). Vendor engagement needed to confirm certification roadmap.

dimensions.name: Organizational Dna
dimensions.summary: Trellix operates a partner program (resellers, MSSPs, technology partners) inherited from McAfee Enterprise and FireEye. The platform is API-open with SIEM/SOAR integrations, marketplace connectors, and documented APIs — Integration Maturity is strong. As a large enterprise cybersecurity company, engagement is workable but not frictionless. The STG private equity ownership adds some uncertainty about strategic direction.

evidence.claim: **Inherited Partner Network | Strength:** Trellix inherited the McAfee Enterprise and FireEye partner ecosystems — a substantial network of MSSPs, resellers, and technology partners. This partner network is a delivery channel for partner-facing lab consumption and a source of channel enablement demand. — Trellix company background (McAfee Enterprise + FireEye merger via STG). Large inherited partner network accelerates channel enablement motion.

**Open API Platform | Strength:** Trellix positions its platform as 'open' with documented APIs, SIEM/SOAR integrations, and third-party connectors — the Intelligence as a Service product is inherently API-first. Open platform architecture confirms that API-based lab scoring and integration scenarios are viable. — Trellix homepage (open platform positioning). API openness is a prerequisite for rich lab scoring; confirmed here.

**Private Equity Ownership | Context:** Trellix is owned by Symphony Technology Group (STG) — a private equity firm. PE ownership can accelerate decision-making (cost-conscious, ROI-focused) or complicate it (strategic uncertainty, budget constraints). Engagement is workable but the PE context should inform how Skillable frames ROI. — Trellix company background. Frame lab investment in terms of customer retention and renewal revenue, not just training quality.

dimensions.name: Delivery Capacity
dimensions.summary: Trellix has authorized training partners and a channel network inherited from McAfee and FireEye. Public ILT courses and eLearning catalog confirm delivery infrastructure. No confirmed LMS platform or existing lab platform identified in research. The ATP network provides a delivery channel for partner-facing lab consumption.

evidence.claim: **Authorized Training Partners | Strength:** Trellix Education Services references authorized training partners and private classroom delivery — an ATP network inherited from McAfee and FireEye provides a channel for partner-delivered lab consumption. — Trellix Education Services page. ATP network is a multiplier for lab consumption volume beyond direct Trellix delivery.

products.name: Trellix Global Threat Intelligence
lab_concepts: Threat Intelligence Triage: Interpreting GTI Reputation Scores
Configuring GTI Integration in Trellix ePO
recommendation: GTI cannot be provisioned as a standalone lab product — it is a SaaS intelligence feed with no installable component, no per-learner tenant, and no learner-facing configuration surface. The correct delivery path is to include GTI as a module within a broader Trellix platform lab: Trellix ePO or Endpoint Security installed on a Hyper-V VM, with GTI integration enabled and validated as part of the lab workflow. Simulation is the only standalone GTI path, and it limits scoring to AI Vision and MCQ. Skillable's recommendation is to scope a Trellix platform lab (ePO, Endpoint Security, or Helix) and treat GTI as one of several integrated components — not to build a GTI-only lab.

Standalone GTI simulation labs are limited to AI Vision scoring (screen state evaluation of displayed reputation results) and MCQ knowledge checks on threat intelligence concepts. The higher-value scoring path is to build GTI as a component within a Trellix ePO or Endpoint Security lab on Hyper-V, where PowerShell or REST API calls can validate that GTI integration is correctly configured, lookups return expected results, and policy enforcement reflects GTI data — enabling script-based or API-based scoring with real outcome validation.

The key question that unblocks this engagement is whether Trellix Education Services plans standalone GTI training or always teaches GTI as part of a broader platform curriculum. The champion should reach out to the Trellix Education Services team and ask: 'Do you currently have or plan standalone GTI training courses, or is GTI always covered as part of ePO or Endpoint Security courses? And do you have an NFR or demo license arrangement for GTI that could support lab authoring?' The answer determines whether a GTI-specific lab is worth scoping or whether the conversation should pivot to the broader Trellix platform.

Trellix Education Services explicitly references hands-on lab exercises across its security training curriculum — this is a greenfield Skillable opportunity on the broader Trellix platform, not GTI specifically. The right entry point is a conversation about Trellix ePO, Endpoint Security, or Helix labs, where Skillable's Hyper-V fabric and scoring capabilities directly address the hands-on training Trellix already delivers in private classrooms. GTI becomes a natural module within that curriculum rather than a standalone product.

Score Trellix ePO (ePolicy Orchestrator) or Trellix Endpoint Security as the primary lab product — both install on Windows Server VMs (Hyper-V), have rich admin consoles with multi-step configuration workflows, and consume GTI as an integrated component. These products have the provisioning depth, configuration complexity, and scoring surface that GTI alone lacks. The Trellix platform as a whole is a strong Skillable candidate; GTI in isolation is not.

dimensions.name: Provisioning
dimensions.summary: GTI is a cloud-delivered SaaS threat intelligence feed with no installable component and no per-learner tenant provisioning. Simulation is the only viable standalone lab path. The saas_only and multi_tenant_only flags apply — GTI cannot be isolated per learner and has no self-hosted deployment option.

evidence.claim: **SaaS Feed Architecture | Risk:** GTI is a cloud-delivered reputation and threat intelligence service with no installable component, no per-learner tenant, and no self-hosted deployment option — simulation is the only viable standalone lab path, limiting learner interactivity and scoring fidelity — Trellix product page (trellix.com). This means any standalone GTI lab cannot provide real hands-on configuration experience.

**No Standalone Install | Blocker:** GTI has no installable server component, no VM image, no container, and no per-learner cloud provisioning path — it is consumed as a backend feed by other Trellix products (ePO, Endpoint Security, Network Security) rather than configured directly by learners — Trellix product listing (trellix.com). A standalone GTI lab cannot be provisioned on any Skillable fabric.

**Platform Context Opportunity | Opportunity:** GTI's instructional value is best realized as a module within a broader Trellix platform lab — Trellix ePO and Endpoint Security install on Windows Server VMs (Hyper-V) and consume GTI feeds, allowing learners to observe GTI-enriched detections in a real environment — Trellix Education Services (trellix.com). This is the recommended path: score the broader Trellix platform, not GTI in isolation.

dimensions.name: Lab Access
dimensions.summary: GTI access is via API key tied to a Trellix product license — there is no learner-facing login portal or per-learner account provisioning. Trial or demo API access may be available but is not designed for lab-scale concurrent use. No credential pool mechanism exists for GTI standalone.

evidence.claim: **API Key Access Only | Risk:** GTI access is gated by a Trellix product license and API key — there is no learner-facing login portal, no per-learner account provisioning mechanism, and no confirmed free trial tier for GTI specifically — Trellix product page (trellix.com). Lab-scale concurrent access would require vendor engagement to establish a shared demo key or NFR arrangement.

**Enterprise License Gating | Risk:** GTI is bundled with Trellix enterprise security products rather than sold as a standalone subscription with a free or NFR tier — no developer or non-production license tier for GTI was found — Trellix product listing (trellix.com). Vendor engagement is required before any lab authoring can begin.

dimensions.name: Scoring
dimensions.summary: GTI exposes a REST API for reputation lookups that could theoretically be queried to validate learner actions, but the primary scoring method for any GTI-adjacent simulation lab is AI Vision (screen state evaluation) or MCQ. No rich programmatic scoring surface exists for standalone GTI labs.

evidence.claim: **Screen-State Scoring Only | Risk:** GTI simulation labs would rely on AI Vision to evaluate whether learners correctly identified threat categories, reputation scores, or IOC classifications from displayed results — no programmatic API scoring surface exists for standalone GTI interactions — Trellix product documentation (trellix.com). This limits scoring granularity to observable UI states rather than validated configuration outcomes.

**Knowledge Check Fallback | Context:** Threat intelligence interpretation concepts (IOC types, reputation scoring, threat categories, false positive triage) are well-suited to knowledge-check questions as a scoring fallback — Trellix Education Services curriculum (trellix.com). MCQ scoring is viable but does not validate hands-on skill.

dimensions.name: Teardown
dimensions.summary: Simulation environments have no persistent state to clean up. Teardown is not a concern for GTI simulation labs — no provisioned resources, accounts, or data are created per learner session.

evidence.claim: **No Teardown Burden | Strength:** Simulation environments create no persistent provisioned resources, accounts, or data per learner — teardown is not a concern for GTI simulation labs — Skillable platform architecture (inferred). Session end automatically clears all simulation state.

dimensions.name: Product Complexity
dimensions.summary: GTI as a standalone product has an extremely thin configuration surface — it is a reputation lookup feed, not a configurable platform. Learners can query it, interpret results, and observe how it enriches connected products, but there are no multi-step workflows, policy configurations, or deployment decisions specific to GTI itself. The Simple UX penalty applies heavily. Instructional value exists only in the context of a broader Trellix platform lab.

evidence.claim: **Minimal Configuration Surface | Risk:** GTI is a reputation lookup and threat feed service — the learner-facing interaction is limited to querying IOC reputation, viewing threat categories, and observing enriched alerts in connected products. There are no multi-step configuration workflows, policy decisions, or deployment tasks specific to GTI itself — Trellix product listing (trellix.com). Standalone GTI labs would have very low hands-on depth.

**Cross-Product Integration Value | Opportunity:** GTI's instructional value emerges when learners configure Trellix endpoint, network, and email products to consume GTI feeds and observe how intelligence enriches detections — this integration workflow is teachable within a broader Trellix platform lab — Trellix product listing (trellix.com). The complexity lives in the consuming products, not GTI itself.

dimensions.name: Mastery Stakes
dimensions.summary: Correctly interpreting threat intelligence data is a high-stakes skill — misreading GTI reputation scores can result in missed detections or blocking legitimate traffic. However, the stakes attach to the analyst skill of threat intelligence interpretation broadly, not to GTI-specific product mastery. GTI itself has minimal configuration where errors cause harm.

evidence.claim: **Threat Intel Interpretation Stakes | Context:** Misreading GTI reputation scores or misconfiguring which products consume GTI data can result in missed threat detections or blocking legitimate traffic — real operational consequences — Trellix Education Services (trellix.com). However, these stakes attach to the broader analyst skill set and platform configuration, not to GTI-specific product mastery.

dimensions.name: Lab Versatility
dimensions.summary: One lab type applies: Incident Response — GTI data enriches incident investigation workflows where analysts must correlate IOC reputation data with active alerts. This is only meaningful as a component of a broader security operations lab, not a standalone GTI experience.

evidence.claim: **GTI-Enriched IR Scenario | Opportunity:** GTI reputation data enriches incident response workflows — a lab scenario where analysts must correlate IOC reputation scores with active alerts to triage and respond to a simulated attack is the most natural GTI lab type — Trellix Education Services curriculum (trellix.com). This scenario requires a broader Trellix platform environment, not GTI standalone.

dimensions.name: Market Demand
dimensions.summary: Cybersecurity is a high-demand training category (+8). GTI uses ML-based threat classification, qualifying as an AI-powered product (+3). Trellix has a large enterprise install base across 40,000+ customers (+2). The threat intelligence subcategory is growing (+1). Market demand is real but applies to the broader Trellix platform, not GTI specifically.

evidence.claim: **Cybersecurity Training Demand | Strength:** Threat intelligence is a core cybersecurity discipline with strong and growing demand for hands-on training — security analysts and SOC teams are among the most lab-hungry personas in enterprise IT — Trellix CyberThreat Report (trellix.com). Market demand is real but applies to the cybersecurity category broadly, not GTI as a standalone product.

**ML-Based Threat Classification | Context:** GTI uses machine learning to classify threats, score reputation, and identify emerging attack patterns across Trellix's global sensor network — Trellix product page (trellix.com). AI features add training relevance but do not create a hands-on configuration surface for learners in GTI itself.

**Large Enterprise Install Base | Strength:** Trellix serves 40,000+ customers across the platform — a significant population of security professionals who consume GTI as part of their Trellix deployment and represent a training audience for GTI-adjacent curriculum — Trellix homepage (trellix.com). Training demand exists within the existing customer base.

dimensions.name: Training Commitment
dimensions.summary: Trellix Education Services offers a broad portfolio including product training, ILT, eLearning, and explicitly hands-on lab exercises. Security training tracks cover penetration testing, forensics, and incident response — confirming a culture of technical, hands-on learning. Training appears to be a meaningful investment, not a checkbox function.

evidence.claim: **Broad Training Portfolio | Strength:** Trellix Education Services offers product training, ILT, eLearning, and security training tracks covering penetration testing, forensics, incident response, and ICS/IoT — explicitly including hands-on lab exercises — Trellix Education Services page (trellix.com). This confirms a mature training function with technical depth.

**Hands-On Lab Emphasis | Strength:** Trellix training descriptions explicitly reference 'hands-on lab exercises' and 'interactive demonstrations' across multiple security training tracks — Trellix Education Services page (trellix.com). This signals organizational alignment with lab-based learning, making Skillable a natural conversation.

dimensions.name: Organizational Dna
dimensions.summary: Trellix operates a channel partner program with MSSPs and resellers. GTI and the broader Trellix platform expose REST APIs and integrate with third-party SIEMs and security tools, indicating open platform orientation. As a mid-large enterprise cybersecurity vendor, Trellix is workable to engage but not a small, agile partner.

evidence.claim: **Channel Partner Program | Context:** Trellix operates a channel partner program including MSSPs, resellers, and technology partners — Trellix homepage (trellix.com). A partner ecosystem creates downstream training demand but the depth and formality of partner enablement for GTI specifically is unconfirmed.

**Open Platform with REST APIs | Strength:** The Trellix Security Platform exposes REST APIs and integrates with third-party SIEMs, SOAR platforms, and security tools — Trellix platform page (trellix.com). Open platform orientation is a positive signal for lab buildability on the broader Trellix product suite.

dimensions.name: Delivery Capacity
dimensions.summary: Trellix Education Services has public ILT and eLearning catalogs with scheduled offerings. Authorized training partners exist. No confirmed Skillable, Instruqt, or CloudShare lab platform integration detected. Delivery infrastructure is present but lab-specific delivery capacity is unconfirmed.

Delivery infrastructure exists and is actively used, providing a channel for lab-enhanced courses.

**No Confirmed Lab Platform | Opportunity:** No existing Skillable, Instruqt, CloudShare, or other hands-on lab platform integration was detected for Trellix — Trellix Education Services page (trellix.com). key_technical_questions.heading: Key Technical Questions
bullets: Find the VP of Education Services or VP of Customer Education at Trellix (likely under the Chief Customer Officer or Chief Revenue Officer org) and ask: 'How are you currently delivering hands-on product training for Trellix XDR and endpoint security modules — are customers and partners getting live environment access, or are they relying on simulations and videos?'

Engage the Director of Partner Enablement and ask: 'When a partner needs to validate competency on Trellix's threat detection and response workflows, what does that lab environment look like today — and how are you managing the infrastructure overhead of spinning up realistic security scenarios at scale?'

conversation_starters.heading: Conversation Starters
bullets: Trellix's platform spans endpoint, email, network, and cloud — that's a complex, multi-surface product that's nearly impossible to teach through slides alone. Skillable lets Trellix deliver scored, hands-on labs where customers and partners actually configure detection rules, triage alerts, and run response playbooks in a safe, instrumented environment — proving competency instead of just completing a course.

Cybersecurity training has a credibility problem: learners watch a demo, pass a quiz, and still can't operate the product under pressure. With an instructional value score of 89 for Trellix's content complexity, Skillable's lab platform is purpose-built to close that gap — giving Trellix Education a measurable way to show customers are truly ready to defend with the platform.

account_intelligence.heading: Account Intelligence
bullets: Trellix was formed in 2022 from the merger of McAfee Enterprise and FireEye — meaning their education and enablement org is still maturing and likely consolidating two legacy training programs into one unified curriculum, which is a prime moment to modernize lab delivery infrastructure before technical debt sets in.

Trellix has been aggressively expanding its partner ecosystem and MSSP channel as part of its go-to-market strategy, which puts Partner Enablement under real pressure to certify partners faster and at scale — a direct use case for Skillable's hybrid Hyper-V deployment, which can support the complex, multi-tenant network environments Trellix security scenarios require.

Trellix's AI-powered XDR positioning means their product is evolving rapidly, and training content has to keep pace — Skillable's lab authoring model allows Education teams to update live lab environments without rebuilding entire courses, which is a concrete operational advantage worth naming in the first conversation.
